name: Rive Deep-Dive — State Machines + Interactive + Free Tier
definition_origin: Rive (rive.app) is a vector animation tool + binary file format (.riv) + cross-platform runtime, designed from day one for INTERACTIVE animations driven by state machines and runtime data. Where Lottie encodes a linear timeline, Rive encodes a graph: states (idle/hover/pressed/loading/error) connected by transitions with conditions, fired by runtime inputs (booleans, numbers, triggers). Originally launched in 2018 and fully rewritten with state machines in 2020. Acquired by Canva in February 2026 — at that point Rive announced free access for individuals including commercial use [uncertain — verify on rive.app/pricing].
core_purpose: Rive's job is interactive motion: animations that respond to user gestures, app state, sensor data, or scroll position. Visceral level: an avatar that turns its head to follow your tap, a button that morphs through five states without ever cutting between sprites. Behavioral level: state-driven UI feedback (toggle ON/OFF with anticipation, drag handles that feel physical). Reflective level: branded interactive characters (Cluely/Linear-style detailed mascots that feel sentient because they react). Rive's superpower over Lottie is that the animation IS the state machine — same file shipped to RN, web, Flutter, iOS native, Android native, and they all read the same .riv with the same state logic.
when_needed: Consumer mobile: gamified apps (fitness streaks, language learning), avatars/mascots that react to user, drag-and-drop UI, scroll-driven scenes. B2B/dev-tool: less common — interactive feature-flag toggles, animated status indicators reacting to telemetry. AI-wrapper: chat assistant 'thinking/listening/speaking' state mascot. Marketing surfaces: hero scene that responds to mouse position, demo of interactive features.
format_options: Single output format: **.riv** (binary). Designed-once → runs everywhere via runtime libraries. The .riv contains: artboards (canvases), animations (timeline tracks), state machines (graph of states + transitions + conditions), inputs (boolean / number / trigger), and data binding (newer 2025+ feature). Compared to Lottie JSON: 10-15x smaller because binary not JSON, schema-versioned, designed for runtime parsing speed. Rive Editor exports → .riv → consumed by runtime which parses the binary and renders via a custom Skia-based GPU pipeline (Metal on iOS, Vulkan on Android, WebGL/WebGPU on web). NOTE: Free tier historically gated EXPORTS — you could design but not ship. Post-Canva acquisition this changed [uncertain — verify].
file_size_performance: Target: <30KB for an icon-sized state-machine asset, <150KB for a character mascot, <500KB for a complex interactive scene. Rive files are typically 10-15x smaller than equivalent Lottie (e.g. 240KB Lottie → 16KB Rive in published benchmark). Runtime cost: GPU-accelerated, ~60fps even with many objects in motion (Lottie hits ~17fps in same RN benchmark). Memory: in published Android benchmark Rive used 25MB native + 184MB graphics for a heavy scene; Lottie used 49MB native + 123MB graphics. Rive trades graphics RAM for native RAM — generally faster CPU but heavier GPU. Optimization: avoid raster images embedded in .riv (use vector); reuse symbols; flatten unused artboards before export.
free_tools: 1) **Rive Editor** (rive.app/editor) — free for individuals; in-browser + desktop apps. Pre-Canva acquisition the free tier had 3 file limit + gated exports. Post-Feb 2026 Canva acquisition: free tier reportedly opened up for individuals including commercial export [uncertain — verify rive.app/pricing]. 2) **Rive Community** (rive.app/community) — free remixable .riv files from other creators (CC-licensed); excellent for learning state-machine patterns. 3) **Rive Marketplace** — free + paid templates. 4) **Rive Help docs + Masterclass blog** — free tutorials. 5) **rive-app/rive-react-native** (npm) — free MIT-licensed runtime. 6) **@rive-app/canvas / @rive-app/webgl** — free web runtimes. 7) **rive-flutter** — free Flutter runtime. ALTERNATIVE for free users still gated by Rive: **Lottie + state-machine support** (Lottie added basic state machines in 2024) covers ~60% of Rive use cases without the export gate.
paid_tools: Starter $9/mo: **Rive Cadet** (historical, may be subsumed by Canva pricing post-2026) — small team features. Pro $32/mo: **Rive Voyager** — collaboration, larger file count, advanced exports [uncertain — verify post-acquisition]. Enterprise $120/mo: **Rive Enterprise** — SSO, custom contracts. Post-Canva Feb 2026: paid tiers may now require Canva Studio/Enterprise license rather than standalone Rive subscription [uncertain]. For solo broke dev: post-acquisition free tier should cover everything; pre-acquisition the gated exports were a deal-breaker. ALTERNATIVE if Rive paid tier ever becomes mandatory again: switch to Lottie + state machine support (lottie-state-machine integrations in lottie-react-native).
gemini_pro_workflow: Rive's editor cannot be replaced by Gemini, but Gemini accelerates the upstream art and the state-machine planning. WORKFLOW: 1) Use **Imagen 4** to generate the static character art in multiple poses (idle, happy, surprised, thinking, error). Prompt: 'Five expression poses of [character], identical proportions and lighting, flat-design vector style, [brand hex palette], transparent background, suitable for vectorization into Rive.' 2) Vectorize each pose via Recraft AI free tier or Inkscape Trace Bitmap. 3) Open Rive Editor, import vector layers, build bone rig (Rive supports skeletal animation). 4) Use **Gemini Pro** in Gemini app to plan the state machine: 'Design a state machine for an avatar with inputs [tapped: trigger, mood: number 0-100], states [idle, happy, sad, surprised], transitions and conditions for each.' Output as a state diagram. 5) Implement the state machine in Rive Editor following the plan. 6) Build a **Gem** as a 'Rive State-Machine Designer' — preload Rive state-machine docs + your brand bible — ask the Gem to design state graphs for new features. 7) **NotebookLM** loaded with rive.app docs + this research file lets you ask 'how do I bind the like-button counter to my mascot's happiness?' GEMINI PRO PROMPT for state-graph planning: 'I need a Rive state machine for [feature]. Inputs available: [list]. Desired states: [list]. Output: states, transitions, conditions in pseudocode I can implement in Rive Editor.'
workflow_steps: 1) Decide Rive is the right format (you genuinely need state-driven interactivity, not just linear motion) — 5 min. 2) Generate static character/asset art in Imagen 4 across all needed states — 30 min. 3) Vectorize each state via Recraft / Inkscape — 30 min. 4) Open Rive Editor, create new artboard at target dimensions — 5 min. 5) Import vector layers, organize into bones / nested artboards — 30-60 min. 6) Build base animations (timeline) for each state (idle loop, hover, pressed, success) — 60-120 min. 7) Add state machine: define states, draw transitions, set conditions, attach animations to states — 30-60 min. 8) Add inputs (booleans, numbers, triggers) — 10 min. 9) Test interactively in Rive Editor preview — 10 min. 10) Export .riv (post-Canva-2026 free tier) — 1 min. 11) Drop into apps/mobile/src/assets/rive/ and wire via @rive-app/react-native — 15 min. Total: ~3-6 hours for a moderate state machine on first try; faster on subsequent.
ai_prompt_templates: 1) **Imagen 4 multi-pose prompt**: 'Five expression poses of [character_description], identical proportions / lighting / style, flat-design 2D vector, [brand hex palette: #X #Y #Z #W #K], transparent background, no gradients, suitable for vectorization. Pose 1: idle neutral. Pose 2: happy / smiling. Pose 3: thinking / hand on chin. Pose 4: surprised / wide eyes. Pose 5: sad / drooping. Reference style: Duolingo Duo, Slack avatars.' 2) **Gem state-machine designer prompt**: 'Design a Rive state machine for [use_case]. Inputs available from app: [list with types]. Desired user-perceived states: [list]. Output a graph of states, transitions with conditions, and which animation track to play in each state. Highlight any cyclical transitions and exit conditions.' 3) **Anti-slop counter-prompt**: '...avoid generic chibi proportions, generic SaaS purple-blue, isometric, melted hands, gradient blob backgrounds.' 4) **NotebookLM brand-mascot consistency query**: 'Given my brand archetype [X] and this character bible, list the 5 most important emotional states for the mascot to express, and the easing curve / motion energy for each.'
claude_code_integration: Install: `pnpm add @rive-app/react-native react-native-nitro-modules` (Nitro is required by the modern rive-react-native). For Expo: works in dev client but not Expo Go (requires native module). On web: `pnpm add @rive-app/react-canvas` (or @rive-app/webgl2). Drop .riv files in `apps/mobile/src/assets/rive/`. Component usage: `<Rive resourceName='avatar' stateMachineName='AvatarStateMachine' style={{width: 200, height: 200}} ref={riveRef} />`. Drive state via ref: `riveRef.current?.setInputState('AvatarStateMachine', 'tapped', true)` or `riveRef.current?.fireState('AvatarStateMachine', 'celebrate')`. For a typed wrapper, generate TS types from the .riv input metadata. Claude Code can scaffold a `<MascotAvatar mood={...} onTap={...} />` wrapper that maps app state to Rive inputs cleanly.
emotional_design_integration: Magician archetype: Rive's strongest pairing — interactive transformation IS the magic. Linear's status icons morphing between states is the canonical example. Jester: gamified bouncy interactive characters (Duolingo would use Rive if rebuilding now — owl that follows your tap, reacts to streak loss). Caregiver: gentle reactive mascot that breathes idle and softly responds to taps (meditation app companion). Hero: fast-snappy state changes for confidence (success badge that flexes when earned). Outlaw: glitchy state transitions for edginess. Avoid Rive for purely Sage brands — its interactivity reads as 'too playful' for serious B2B/finance. Across all archetypes, the state-machine design IS the brand voice: how does your mascot react to error? With apology (Caregiver), shrug (Outlaw), or determined retry (Hero)?
specific_examples: 1) **Cluely / Linear-style interactive mascots** — engineered with Rive state machines and runtime architecture; the dev.to writeup walks through the production setup. https://dev.to/uianimation/engineering-interactive-mascots-with-rives-state-machine-and-runtime-architecture-4e2h 2) **Valley Adventures (Codrops case study)** — full Rive React integration for an interactive marketing scene. https://tympanus.net/codrops/2025/05/12/integrating-rive-into-a-react-project-behind-the-scenes-of-valley-adventures/ 3) **Rive's own marketing site (rive.app)** — every interactive demo on the page is a .riv, dogfooding the format. 4) **Duolingo (experimental)** — has been migrating select character animations from Lottie to Rive for the reactive 'tap to celebrate' interactions [uncertain — verify]. 5) **Figma's status indicators** in some surfaces. 6) **Rive Community page** itself has thousands of free production examples to remix: https://rive.app/community.
anti_ai_slop_traps: Rive-specific slop tells: (a) **Default Rive demo characters** — the bear, the cube, the bouncy ball that ship with Rive Editor templates. Every newbie ships them as-is. Counter: always retexture, recolor, or rebuild — never ship demo-asset. (b) **Generic 'tap to celebrate' confetti** — every gamified app ends up with the same Rive confetti cannon. Counter: confetti shape/color/timing must match brand (e.g. Caregiver brand should use soft floating petals, not explosive confetti). (c) **Over-engineered state machine** — 47 states for what should be 4. Counter: design state machine on paper first, max 8 states for any single asset. (d) **Mascot that reacts to literally everything** — feels needy, drains battery. Counter: mascot reacts only to meaningful moments (success, milestone, error), idle otherwise. (e) **Imagen 4 generated character with inconsistent proportions across poses** — pose 1 has big head, pose 3 has small head, character looks like 5 different beings. Counter: explicit 'identical proportions and lighting' in every Imagen prompt; better yet, generate one canonical pose and warp/redraw in Rive's bone rigging system. NEGATIVE PROMPT for Imagen 4 source: 'no chibi, no isometric, no inconsistent proportions, no melted features, no generic SaaS aesthetics.'
common_pitfalls: 1) Treating Rive like Lottie (just play and forget) — wastes 90% of the value. If you don't need state machines, use Lottie (faster to author). 2) Building 47-state machines that no one understands, including future-you. Keep ≤8 states per asset. 3) Forgetting to test on Android — Rive's Android runtime had performance regressions historically; verify on real devices. 4) Bundling huge .riv files in app instead of OTA delivery — bloats app store size. 5) Not using prefers-reduced-motion — Rive's interactivity must degrade gracefully. 6) Tight runtime coupling — every screen calling riveRef directly creates spaghetti; build a `<Mascot mood={...} />` wrapper that maps app state to Rive inputs. 7) Pre-Feb-2026: building everything in Rive free tier and discovering you can't export — verify post-acquisition pricing before committing.
accessibility_notes: Container needs aria-label describing the mascot/asset purpose. Provide a static-image fallback for prefers-reduced-motion (export a still PNG from Rive Editor of the idle state and swap at runtime). Interactive Rive elements that are functional buttons need keyboard focus + Enter/Space activation — wrap in <Pressable> with proper accessibilityRole='button' and accessibilityLabel. Do not rely on motion alone to convey state changes — pair with text or icon updates that screen readers can announce.
mobile_vs_web_diffs: Mobile (RN): @rive-app/react-native + react-native-nitro-modules; native runtimes call Rive's Skia-based renderer through Metal (iOS) / Vulkan or GLES (Android). Bundled in app or OTA delivery. Web: @rive-app/react-canvas (CPU-canvas) or @rive-app/webgl2 (GPU). On web, lazy-load the runtime via dynamic import; on mobile it's already part of the app bundle. Web runtime is ~200KB gzipped which is a real cost — only include if you ship Rive content on the page.
sources: 1) [Rive Pricing](https://rive.app/pricing) — verify post-Canva-acquisition tiers. 2) [Rive React Native runtime](https://rive.app/docs/runtimes/react-native) — official integration guide. 3) [Rive State Machines Guide](https://help.rive.app/runtimes/state-machines). 4) [Rive vs Lottie blog](https://rive.app/blog/rive-as-a-lottie-alternative) — first-party comparison. 5) [Callstack: Lottie vs Rive Mobile Animation](https://www.callstack.com/blog/lottie-vs-rive-optimizing-mobile-app-animation) — RN benchmark numbers. 6) [Codrops: Integrating Rive into React](https://tympanus.net/codrops/2025/05/12/integrating-rive-into-a-react-project-behind-the-scenes-of-valley-adventures/) — production case. 7) [Engineering Interactive Mascots with Rive](https://dev.to/uianimation/engineering-interactive-mascots-with-rives-state-machine-and-runtime-architecture-4e2h) — implementation deep-dive. 8) [Rive Masterclass: Rive vs Lottie 2026](https://www.rivemasterclass.com/blog/rive-vs-lottie).
